text: Docomomo US provides a historic overview of the Washington Dulles International Airport Main Terminal. It states that Eero Saarinen and Associates were chosen as architects in 1958 and that Ammann & Whitney served as engineers. It reports construction began in 1958 and that the airport officially opened and was dedicated by President John F. Kennedy on November 17, 1962. It describes the terminal’s concrete panel roof supported by catenary cables to create an uninterrupted interior space supporting passenger flow to mobile lounges. It notes the terminal received a 1966 AIA Honor Award and was determined eligible for the National Register of Historic Places in 1978.

MWAA’s overview page describes Dulles’s current layout, stating it has a Main Terminal and four midfield concourses (A, B, C, D) served by AeroTrain and mobile lounges. It states the Main Terminal is eligible for listing on the National Register of Historic Places. It gives approximate facility sizes, including about 1.3 million square feet for the Main Terminal and about 268,000 square feet for the International Arrivals Building, with capacity up to 2,400 passengers per hour.

MWAA’s flydulles airport overview describes the original terminal configuration and later expansions. It states the terminal was originally a compact two-level structure 600 feet long and 200 feet wide designed by Eero Saarinen, and that it was later expanded in accordance with Saarinen’s original design to 1,240 feet in length and about 1.1 million square feet. It also states that when the airport opened in 1962, passengers walked only about 200 feet from entering the terminal until they were seated in a mobile lounge to travel to their aircraft.

An FAA page about revitalizing Washington Dulles International Airport describes a December 2025 request for information seeking approaches for new terminal(s) and concourses that could replace the existing terminal and concourses. It states that proposals may retain portions of existing facilities by incorporating all or part of the historic Eero Saarinen-designed main terminal into a new terminal building.

WTTW’s companion article for the series “10 Buildings That Changed America” profiles Dulles International Airport as designed by Eero Saarinen and completed in 1962. It states construction began in 1958 and that Saarinen died in 1961 before completion. It describes the terminal’s catenary-curve roof structure enabling an open hall without interior columns and discusses the mobile lounge concept for transporting passengers from terminal to aircraft.

A Ghosts of DC article narrates the development of Dulles Airport, including site selection and land acquisition, and provides a construction timeline and cost figure. It states that in 1958 President Eisenhower decided on the western Fairfax County site and that landowners received condemnation letters for about 10,000 acres at $500 per acre. It describes Saarinen’s terminal concept and the mobile lounge system. It also states that construction ran from 1959 to 1962 and cost $110 million.

Airport-Technology’s project profile states that the main terminal designed by Eero Saarinen opened in 1962 and notes that it was later expanded to about 1.1 million square feet in 1996 in line with the original design. The page primarily focuses on later development projects.

A Historypin entry presents an archival construction photograph captioned as showing Washington Dulles International Airport under construction, and states the main terminal was designed in 1958 by architect Eero Saarinen. The entry credits the image to the Library of Virginia’s collection via an “Engineering in Virginia” association and includes a 1962 date in metadata.

MWAA’s Dulles Corridor Metrorail timeline/history page provides contextual narrative linking Dulles Airport’s opening era with later regional transportation projects such as the Dulles Toll Road and the Metrorail Silver Line. It serves as background context rather than detailed evidence about the terminal’s construction costs or engineering.

Ray Clark’s academic paper analyzes the design process of the Washington Dulles International Airport terminal using research in the Eero Saarinen Archives at Yale. It reports that Saarinen’s office conducted extensive precedent study by visiting 18 U.S. airports and three foreign airports (Vancouver, Gatwick, Frankfurt), collecting information on layout and passenger flows and reviewing architectural journal discussions about terminal innovations. It frames the terminal design debate as prioritizing air carriers’ operational needs versus air travelers’ experience and discusses how this influenced decisions such as minimizing walking distances, using mobile lounges, and creating a unified terminal hall. It characterizes Dulles as a new-build airport planned for the jet age.

A Simple Flying article summarizes the design significance of Dulles’s terminal and states that construction began in 1958 and was completed in 1962. It describes the roof form associated with Saarinen and explains the mobile lounge concept as part of the intended passenger flow model.

text: 该文介绍华盛顿杜勒斯国际机场主航站楼为Eero Saarinen设计、1962年启用，并提到Ammann & Whitney作为工程协作方。文中强调航站楼“喷气时代”语境下的纪念性造型与功能组织：一条线性的大厅空间配合“移动候机室（mobile lounges）”而非指廊式登机廊。其屋盖为强烈曲线形态，由倾斜的钢筋混凝土支撑构件与电缆（悬索/悬链线）体系共同受力；立面采用大面积玻璃幕墙并在大挑檐下后退布置，以引入采光并保持室内外视觉连续。材料描述集中在钢筋混凝土、钢与大玻璃面板，室内强调开敞与流线清晰，并将其置于Saarinen具雕塑性的现代主义作品谱系中。

该PDF以杜勒斯主航站楼作为悬索（电缆）支承屋盖的结构案例进行讲解，归功Eero Saarinen为建筑师。内容描述屋盖由两排倾斜钢筋混凝土墩/框架支承，钢索在其间形成近似悬链线曲线并与地基/锚固系统共同工作，将屋面荷载传递至混凝土构件与基础。材料与构造层面提到钢索体系与相对薄的混凝土屋面板（或壳/板）在索与次结构之上成型，从而实现大跨度与室内大空间；并讨论倾斜支承对水平分力的抵抗、基础埋置、以及整体稳定（如屋盖的整体作用等）。

该页面将杜勒斯国际机场（1962，Chantilly, VA）归于Eero Saarinen及其事务所，并称其为Saarinen晚期重要作品。页面描述主航站楼为长条形体量与“漂浮感”曲面屋盖，屋盖由倾斜钢筋混凝土支撑与钢索悬挂系统支承，从而形成开敞、少柱或无柱的室内空间，并提及玻璃幕墙与外部混凝土结构节奏。文本将其与Saarinen的其他项目（如TWA航站楼）并置，强调其具有喷气时代的未来感与雕塑化的现代主义特征，并指出其平面与交通组织与移动候机室概念相关。

该PBS American Masters条目以“革命性设计”解读杜勒斯主航站楼，强调Saarinen在喷气时代对机场类型学的重构：以一个宏大的单体大厅配合移动候机室替代传统长指廊，从而应对旅客与喷气机时代运营需求。文中将扫掠式（抛物/曲线）屋盖与倾斜混凝土支撑描述为表达速度、运动与技术乐观主义的形体语言，并强调屋盖在玻璃围护上方呈“悬浮”效果带来的开敞与未来感。条目还提到Saarinen将建筑、工业设计与图形系统（如标识、家具与车辆）作为整体环境进行整合，使该航站楼成为中世纪现代主义与喷气时代文化象征。

该FAA环境评估附件以历史/建筑资源章节描述杜勒斯主航站楼的建筑特征与历史意义。文件将主航站楼识别为具有历史与建筑重要性的资源，指出其由Eero Saarinen设计、1962年完成，Ammann & Whitney为咨询工程师，并概述其主要设计要素：东西向延展的航站楼体量、径向的电缆支承屋盖、倾斜的钢筋混凝土支撑构件、玻璃幕墙以及开敞的内部空间。文中提及其作为20世纪中期现代主义的重要例证，并讨论机场设施随时间演进及改造，同时表示主航站楼的外部形体与主要材料（混凝土、玻璃、钢）总体保持。文件亦联系其最初对移动候机室系统的适配，以及后续由廊道/列车等系统补充或部分替代。

该书（Brian Edwards，《The Modern Airport Terminal – New Approaches to Airport Architecture》）将杜勒斯作为现代机场航站楼的早期经典案例，讨论其作为喷气时代“目的性设计”转折点的意义。书中概述主航站楼由Eero Saarinen设计，采用单一纪念性大厅作为处理与集散空间，并从结构与材料角度概括其长跨度屋盖、倾斜混凝土支撑与电缆支承、连续玻璃围护以及钢筋混凝土、钢、玻璃等材料组合；并将其作为“整体设计（total design）”思路的一部分进行比较分析。内容还提到移动候机室概念对灵活停机位组织的设想及其在后期交通增长下的效率问题，并将其与后来的枢纽机场组织方式作对照。
该Modernist Collection条目以设计史口吻介绍杜勒斯机场由Eero Saarinen设计、1962年启用，强调其室内与整体风格的未来感与喷气时代审美。文本指出主航站楼室内被作为统一的现代主义构成来处理，包含家具、柜台、标识与照明等与结构形态相呼应的设计，并将其与Saarinen其他作品的风格谱系联系，突出开敞体量、清晰线条与材料/色彩策略对“现代技术与效率”形象的强化。
该AIA Journal（1981年11月号）PDF中包含一处与杜勒斯国际机场相关的简短文字/图注信息，指出“Dulles International Airport, Chantilly, Va.”由“Kevin Roche John Dinkeloo & Associates”于1962年完成，用于说明项目在Saarinen去世后由其事务所继任团队完成的事实线索。

PBS条目将扫掠式曲面屋盖与倾斜混凝土支撑解释为表达速度、运动与现代技术的形体语言，并将其置于喷气时代文化与中世纪现代主义的象征框架中。
FAA附件在描述主航站楼特征时列举其主要材料与构成要素，包括混凝土（倾斜支撑/构件）、玻璃幕墙与钢（如电缆支承屋盖相关构件），并指出这些主要材料总体保持。

description: 关于屋盖几何/术语表述存在不一致：有来源称其为“radial cable-supported roof”，也有来源以“catenary-like cables”或“parabolic/sweeping roof”描述；这些表述可能指向不同层面的几何（索线形态 vs. 屋盖外形 vs. 索的布置方式），但在同一对象（主航站楼屋盖体系）上用词不同，需在入图时区分“索线曲线类型”“索的平面布置（径向/非径向）”与“屋盖外形”。

text: MWAA/FlyDulles的D2项目页面介绍“Main Terminal Improvement Project”，并给出“Date Completed: 2005”和“Program Cost: $174.2 million”等项目基本信息，用于描述该主航站楼改进项目作为Dulles Development (D2)资本项目的一部分。
MWAA/FlyDulles信息页面包含机场历史与重大项目概述，其中提到1996年MWAA完成了将1962年原始主航站楼扩建至其设计长度的工程，并将其描述为一项建筑里程碑。

MWAA的D2项目页面描述“Main Terminal AeroTrain Station”作为主航站楼空侧的大型扩建：车站位于地下约60英尺以避免遮挡Saarinen航站楼视线；车站覆盖主航站楼全长（约1,600英尺），设有四个层级与“side-center-side”站台布置。页面还描述安检与客流组织调整：将旅客安检转移到新的Security Mezzanine以缓解此前在售票层安检点的拥堵；Security Mezzanine可从行李提取区与停车设施直接到达。页面给出时间信息：车站与AeroTrain系统于2009年完成，并于2010年1月26日开放。
MWAA的D2项目页面介绍AeroTrain系统为地下自动旅客捷运系统，连接主航站楼与A、B、C登机口区域，并说明其取代了多数此前用于主航站楼与中场廊之间运输旅客的Mobile Lounge功能；页面给出AeroTrain于2010年1月26日对旅客开放。

MWAA关于“US DOT Request for Information to Revitalize Washington Dulles International Airport”的页面在联邦振兴背景下描述Dulles现状设施：机场由主航站楼与四个中场廊（A、B、C、D）组成，可通过地下AeroTrain或mobile lounges到达；并给出主航站楼建筑面积约130万平方英尺、拥有6个Z登机口（其中4个带登机桥）；另描述国际到达楼面积约26.8万平方英尺，可处理最高每小时2,400名旅客，并容纳海关/农业/移民等功能。
MWAA/FlyDulles的机场历史页面回顾Dulles早期设计特点，提到1962年机场对公众而言最独特的特征之一是专门设计的Mobile Lounges，用于运输旅客。
The Transport Politic在AeroTrain开放当日发表文章，讨论AeroTrain启用对Dulles“mobile lounge”体系的影响，主张AeroTrain替代了mobile lounges在主航站楼与中场廊之间运送旅客的主要功能，并从运营与体验角度评论这一转变。
Air Travel Analysis发表的文章以叙事方式回顾Dulles发展历程，涉及mobile lounges、廊道建设以及后续系统演变，并提到mobile lounges在部分场景仍被使用（例如与某些廊区的接驳相关）。

SOM项目页面介绍其在Dulles的工作脉络：自1985年起参与机场总体规划（包含6个中场廊）、国际到达大厅与多种交通网络；并称其在1990年代主导了将主航站楼狭长体量长度“增加到原来的两倍以上”的扩建，强调尊重中世纪现代建筑并整合既有加建；页面还称其构想的AeroTrain系统自2010年开放以来为主航站楼与中场廊之间提供轨道与步行连接。页面元数据包含“Completion Year 2010”和“Design Finish Year 2009”。
Washington Business Journal报道MWAA提出到2034年完成的约220亿美元机场改造计划；摘要信息指出该计划将取消mobile lounges并扩建主航站楼。
Airport Architecture（Edward Russell的Substack）文章描述MWAA向航空公司分享的拟议振兴计划，提到主航站楼扩建、四条新的线性廊道、以及新的地下客流组织（不再使用Direct Jet Transports/移动休息室），目标在2034年前完成。
AviationCircle的Facebook帖子以清单形式概述MWAA约220亿美元重建计划，并在摘要中列出多个组成部分及金额/时间节点，包括约62亿美元的主航站楼扩建与A廊翻新（据称自2027年4月开始）以及与AeroTrain延伸和地下连接相关的项目。
MWAA“History and Facts”页面以概览方式提到D2计划完成的新设施，包括新的空管塔、扩建登机口、第四条跑道与AeroTrain系统等，用于概述MWAA大型资本项目。
MWAA“Development Projects”页面作为项目索引，列出开发项目并包含指向如“Main Terminal AeroTrain Station”等D2项目页面的链接与条目。
MWAA“Business”信息页面提供机场事实信息；摘要信息提到主航站楼具备列入美国国家史迹名录（National Register of Historic Places）的资格。

text: Working scope definition for article: 'Dulles International Airport Main Terminal' is treated as the original Saarinen-designed landside terminal building (the monumental glass-and-concrete hall) and its directly attached landside/airside processing spaces (ticketing, security, baggage claim/arrivals, curbs, and the interface to mobile lounges and later the AeroTrain). Concourses (A/B/C/D) and midfield terminals are described only insofar as they connect to or were enabled by the main terminal. Key factual anchor points already evidenced in the graph: design by Eero Saarinen (with engineering by Ammann & Whitney); opening in 1962 with JFK dedication on 1962-11-17; original circulation concept using mobile lounges; later milestone projects include a 1996 expansion to design length, a 2005 Main Terminal Improvement Project, and a 2009/2010 below-grade AeroTrain station with security-checkpoint relocation to preserve the terminal’s historic sightlines.

title: Lead
content: The Dulles International Airport Main Terminal is the original passenger terminal building at Washington Dulles International Airport, which opened in 1962.[1][2] Designed for the jet age by Eero Saarinen & Associates for the Federal Aviation Agency, the building was conceived as a passenger-processing gateway rather than a conventional gateside concourse, using mobile lounges to carry travelers between the terminal and aircraft.[2][3][4] The terminal’s most prominent architectural feature is a long, curving, cable-supported roof carried by canted reinforced-concrete piers, used to create a large, uninterrupted interior hall.[2][5][6] Over time, the main terminal has been expanded and modernized, including an expansion completed in 1996 and a main-terminal improvement project completed in 2005.[7][8] In 2010, an underground AeroTrain station and automated people-mover system opened at the terminal, adding rail connections to midfield concourses and relocating passenger screening to a new security mezzanine to address congestion.[9][10] The terminal has been described by preservation and cultural institutions as architecturally significant and is eligible for listing in the U.S. National Register of Historic Places.[2][11]

title: History and planning
content:
Planning for what became Washington Dulles International Airport accelerated in the late 1950s as the Washington area sought a new jet-age airport and a terminal concept centered on passenger processing.[12][4] An excerpted institutional history states that a project team was assembled in 1958 to plan, design, build, and open the airport.[13] Docomomo US reports that Eero Saarinen and Associates were chosen as architects in 1958, with Ammann & Whitney selected as engineers.[2][3]

The airport (and its main terminal) opened in 1962.[1][2] Docomomo US states the airport was officially opened and formally dedicated by U.S. President John F. Kennedy on November 17, 1962.[2] MWAA’s later institutional history notes that Washington Dulles International Airport was transferred from federal control to the Metropolitan Washington Airports Authority (MWAA) in 1987, placing the terminal within MWAA’s capital programs for expansion and modernization.[14]

title: Design and architecture
content:
The main terminal was designed by Eero Saarinen & Associates for the Federal Aviation Agency.[2][3] Saarinen did not live to see its completion; he died in 1961, and an architectural reference in AIA Journal indicates the project was completed in 1962 by Kevin Roche John Dinkeloo & Associates, Saarinen’s successor firm.[15][16]

A key architectural and structural feature is the roof system. Docomomo US describes the roof as concrete panels supported by catenary cables, enabling a large, uninterrupted interior space.[2][5] An FAA environmental-assessment appendix similarly identifies a cable-supported roof and notes major material elements including concrete (notably canted support members), glass curtain walls, and steel components associated with the roof-support system.[6][17] A structural-case-study PDF describes the roof as supported by a steel cable system spanning between two rows of canted reinforced-concrete supports, with cables characterized as catenary-like curves and anchored into the ground, and with the inclined supports resisting horizontal forces from the cable system.[18][19]

Multiple sources emphasize the terminal’s long, transparent glass walls set back beneath the roof overhang, contributing to daylight and visual openness.[6][20] Architectural histories have also framed the building as a jet-age, midcentury modern landmark whose monumental hall and sculptural roof form were intended to convey speed, movement, and technological optimism.[21][22]

Sources differ in the technical terminology used to describe the roof geometry and cable arrangement (for example, describing cables as “catenary,” “catenary-like,” or “radial” in plan).[2][6][18] Available evidence does not fully reconcile whether these terms refer to different aspects of the system (cable curve shape versus plan layout), so descriptions in this article follow each cited source’s wording.[2][6][18]

title: Construction and opening
content:
Docomomo US reports that construction of Washington Dulles International Airport began in 1958 and that the airport officially opened and was dedicated by President John F. Kennedy on November 17, 1962.[2] An article from Ghosts of DC states that construction ran from 1959 to 1962 and cost US$110 million.[23] Historypin presents an archival construction photograph captioned as showing Dulles under construction, with metadata indicating a terminal design date of 1958.[24]

These sources differ slightly on the exact year construction began (1958 versus 1959), and no single primary construction contract record is provided in the available material.[2][23] However, all agree that the main terminal was built in the late 1950s and was completed in time for the airport’s 1962 opening and dedication.[1][2][23][24]

title: Renovations and expansions
content:
The main terminal has undergone several major expansions and modernization projects. MWAA’s flydulles overview describes the original terminal as a compact two-level structure 600 feet long and 200 feet wide, designed by Eero Saarinen.[25] The same overview states that the Airports Authority completed an architectural milestone in 1996 by expanding the original 1962 terminal to its design length, increasing the building’s length to 1,240 feet and its floor area to about 1.1 million square feet.[7][25] Later MWAA baseline descriptions and a U.S. Department of Transportation Request for Information (RFI) describe the Main Terminal as having approximately 1.3 million square feet of floor space.[26][27]

The terminal continued to be modernized under the Dulles Development (D2) capital program. A MWAA project page identifies a “Main Terminal Improvement Project” with a completion date of 2005 and a program cost of US$174.2 million, described as part of the broader D2 program.[8] The D2 program’s summary of completed facilities includes the AeroTrain system, a fourth runway, expanded airline gates, and a new air traffic control tower.[28]

A major further expansion on the airfield side of the Main Terminal was the Main Terminal AeroTrain Station. MWAA’s D2 project page describes this as a station constructed approximately 60 feet below ground to avoid obstructing views of Saarinen’s terminal, spanning the entire length of the Main Terminal (about 1,600 feet) and representing a major expansion on the airfield side.[9][10][29] The page states that the station and the AeroTrain System were completed in 2009 and opened on January 26, 2010.[9][29][30] As part of this project, passenger screening operations were planned to move from the ticketing level to a new Security Mezzanine, with MWAA stating that moving screening to this mezzanine would alleviate congestion from the previous screening location and provide direct access from baggage claim areas and parking facilities.[10][31]

MWAA has also undertaken more targeted improvement projects in and under the Main Terminal. A 2018 invitation for bids titled “Main Terminal Baggage Basement Improvements, Dulles Airport” required a contractor to furnish labor, materials, tools, equipment, and supervision to construct improvements to the Main Terminal’s baggage basement; the solicitation was last updated on January 9, 2019.[32]

Recent planning documents describe prospective redevelopment of terminal and concourse facilities. MWAA public notices for a Draft Programmatic Environmental Assessment state that a proposed action includes redevelopment of the Terminal/Concourse facilities at Dulles and associated development.[33][34] A separate MWAA notice records that in May 2026 the Federal Aviation Administration issued a Finding of No Significant Impact/Record of Decision (FONSI/ROD) for terminal concourse redevelopment.[35] Contemporary reporting on MWAA planning, including a Washington Business Journal article and a summary by AviationCircle, describes a plan totaling around US$22 billion through 2034 that would eliminate the use of mobile lounges and expand the Main Terminal, including components such as a US$6.2 billion expansion of the main terminal with renovation of Concourse A starting in April 2027 and a US$3.75 billion AeroTrain extension and underground connector linking the terminal to a new Concourse D.[36][37][38] A Substack article by Airport Architecture similarly characterizes a proposed plan involving terminal expansion, four new linear concourses, new subterranean circulation without Direct Jet Transports (mobile lounges), and completion by 2034.[39]

These future-plan figures and component costs are reported in news and commentary sources and are not fully cross-verified by primary MWAA planning documents in the available evidence.[36][37][38][39] However, they indicate an ongoing planning trajectory in which the historic main terminal would be expanded and integrated with new concourses and circulation systems while phasing out mobile lounges.[26][36][39]

title: Layout and functions
content:
MWAA’s airport overview describes the current layout of Washington Dulles International Airport as consisting of a Main Terminal and four midfield concourses (A, B, C, and D).[26][40] The overview states that passengers can reach the midfield concourses from the Main Terminal via an underground automated people mover (AeroTrain) system or via mobile lounges that transport passengers to and from the Main Terminal.[26][40] It also notes that the airport has six aircraft gates referred to as the Z Gates, associated with the Main Terminal.[26][41]

The same overview and related airport information describe the Main Terminal as having a total of approximately 1.3 million square feet of floor space.[26][27] An RFI page for revitalizing Dulles similarly identifies the Main Terminal’s floor space as about 1.3 million square feet and describes an International Arrivals Building of nearly 268,000 square feet that provides customs, agriculture, and immigration facilities, with capacity for up to 2,400 passengers per hour.[27][42][43]

Official and third-party guides characterize the Main Terminal as a two-level building with departures (ticketing/check-in) on the upper level and arrivals (baggage claim) on the lower level.[25][44][45] Flydulles’ security-information page states that all TSA security checkpoints at Dulles are accessed from the Departures (Ticketing/Check-In) level and identifies three checkpoints: East, West, and TSA PreCheck.[46][47] A layout guide notes that on the upper level there are four ticketing areas along with two primary security checkpoint areas, East and West.[45][48] Information-desk listings place desks on the Departures level (ticketing), on the Arrivals level (baggage claim), and just beyond the East and West security checkpoints, reinforcing this functional division.[49]

The International Arrivals Building is described as a separate facility linked to the main terminal complex, providing customs, agriculture, and immigration services for arriving international passengers and capable of handling up to 2,400 passengers per hour.[27][42][43] Flydulles states that all arriving international passengers and luggage must be screened by U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP), and that passengers connecting onward must re-check luggage and pass through Transportation Security Administration (TSA) screening.[50][51] CBP’s Port of Washington–Dulles contact information identifies the port location as the Main Terminal Lower Level.[52]

The Main Terminal also functions as the hub for landside connections. Flydulles describes the Dulles Airport Metrorail Station, served by the Washington Metro Silver Line, as being connected to the main terminal by an indoor pedestrian tunnel with moving sidewalks.[53][54] The tunnel provides convenient access to the terminal’s ticketing and baggage-claim levels and links the terminal to Parking Garage 1.[54][55][56] Airport maps and information pages provide further details on passenger circulation, including interactive maps and links for security information, “Kiss & Fly,” customs and immigration, and Mobile Passport, but those materials were not fully captured in the available evidence.[57]

Within the airfield system, MWAA documentation notes that the AeroTrain is the automated airport train system for Dulles, connecting passengers between the Main Terminal and the midfield concourses.[58][59] The station in the Main Terminal is located just beyond the TSA security-screening checkpoints, and the underground people mover uses fully automated three-car trains to link the Main Terminal to concourses A, B, and C.[59][60][61] There is no AeroTrain station at Concourse D; passengers can walk there through Concourse C or ride a mobile lounge shuttle.[60][62] MWAA indicates that the airport operates a fleet of 49 mobile lounges, which continue to serve certain connections, particularly where AeroTrain does not provide direct access.[40][63]

Available evidence emphasizes the overall functional organization of the Main Terminal and its connections to other facilities but does not provide complete, authoritative diagrams for all interior spaces or for detailed ground-transportation curb arrangements. As a result, this article focuses on high-level layout and operational roles that are explicitly documented in the cited sources.[25][26][44][57]

title: Operations and evolution of passenger circulation
content:
The original terminal concept at Dulles emphasized minimizing walking distances and using mobile lounges to carry passengers between the Main Terminal and their aircraft.[4][64] MWAA’s history page notes that when the airport opened in 1962, passengers typically walked only about 200 feet from entering the terminal before being seated in a mobile lounge for the trip to the aircraft.[25][65] The same history identifies specially designed mobile lounges as the feature that made the new airport distinctive to the public in 1962.[66] The terminal thus functioned primarily as a passenger-processing gateway, with ticketing, security, and amenities in a central hall feeding mobile-lounge boarding points rather than long concourses at the gate line.[4][21][64]

Over time, this model evolved as new concourses and people-mover systems were added. MWAA’s overview and RFI materials describe the airport as having a Main Terminal and four midfield concourses (A, B, C, and D), reached via an underground automated people mover (AeroTrain) or via mobile lounges.[26][40][67] Flydulles describes AeroTrain as the airport’s automated train system connecting passengers between the Main Terminal and the midfield concourses, using fully automated three-car trains operating in an underground tunnel.[59][60][61] The AeroTrain has been in operation since 2010, with Flydulles specifying that it carries passengers between the terminal and the midfield gates for domestic and international departing flights and domestic arriving flights; most international arriving passengers do not ride AeroTrain because they must first clear U.S. customs.[59][68][69]

Several sources characterize AeroTrain as replacing most of the previous mobile-lounge system for movements between the Main Terminal and the midfield concourses.[29][70][71] At the same time, MWAA’s overview and other guides indicate that mobile lounges remain in service for certain routes, particularly to Concourse D, which does not have an AeroTrain station, and in other specific operational contexts.[40][60][62][63] A Transport Politic commentary on the system’s opening argues that AeroTrain effectively took over the main function of moving passengers between the terminal and the midfield concourses, with mobile lounges playing a reduced but continuing role.[71] This coexistence of systems means that mobile lounges still form part of the Main Terminal’s operational interface with the airfield, even as rail-based circulation has become primary for many passengers.[26][40][59][71]

The opening of the Main Terminal AeroTrain Station also reconfigured passenger screening. MWAA’s project description notes that the station is located just beyond the TSA security-screening checkpoints in the Main Terminal, and that relocating screening to a new Security Mezzanine would alleviate congestion from the previous screening location on the ticketing level.[10][31][59] Flydulles states that all TSA checkpoints (East, West, and TSA PreCheck) are accessed from the Departures (Ticketing/Check-In) level, and that connecting passengers from international flights must re-check luggage and go through TSA screening.[46][47][51]

Customs and immigration processing for international arrivals is handled primarily in the International Arrivals Building, which provides customs, agriculture, and immigration facilities and can accommodate up to 2,400 passengers per hour.[27][42][43] Flydulles notes that all arriving international passengers and luggage must be screened by U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP), and CBP identifies the Port of Washington–Dulles facility as located on the Main Terminal Lower Level, underscoring the integration of federal inspection services within the main-terminal complex.[50][52][72]

Available sources do not provide a comprehensive chronology of all operational changes in the terminal (such as specific post-2001 security modifications or airline-assignment changes), so the account here focuses on elements directly documented in MWAA and other cited materials: the shift from exclusive reliance on mobile lounges to a mix of AeroTrain and lounges, the repositioning of security screening to a mezzanine connected to the AeroTrain station, and the integration of customs and immigration facilities with the main-terminal complex.[26][27][40][59]

title: Cultural significance and critical reception
content:
The Dulles main terminal has been widely discussed in architectural and cultural commentary as a landmark of jet-age and midcentury modern design. Docomomo US characterizes the terminal as an important modern building commissioned by the Federal Aviation Agency, designed by Eero Saarinen & Associates, with a concrete-panel roof supported by catenary cables to create an uninterrupted interior space.[2][3][5] The organization’s register entry notes that the terminal received an American Institute of Architects (AIA) Honor Award in 1966 and that it was determined eligible for listing in the National Register of Historic Places in 1978.[2] MWAA’s overview and business-information pages also state that the Main Terminal is eligible for listing on the National Register of Historic Places.[26][73][74]

The terminal’s cultural resonance has been emphasized in several popular and scholarly discussions. A PBS American Masters feature describes Saarinen’s design as “revolutionary,” presenting the building as a break from earlier airport types by using a single monumental hall and mobile lounges instead of long gate concourses; it interprets the sweeping roof and canted concrete supports as formal expressions of speed, movement, and technological optimism within the jet-age cultural context.[21][22] The same feature notes Saarinen’s integration of architecture, industrial design, and graphic systems (including furnishings, ticket counters, signage, and vehicles) into a unified environment, framing the terminal as a symbol of midcentury modernism and jet-age culture.[21]

Architectural-history publications and retrospectives similarly position Dulles as a significant work within Saarinen’s oeuvre and within airport design more broadly. An architecture-history entry and a Modernist Collection essay both describe the terminal as an iconic late work by Saarinen, highlighting its sculptural roof, canted concrete supports, extensive glass walls, and open interior, and present it as a key example of modernist “total design” in which building form, structure, and interior elements were coordinated to project an image of modern technology and efficiency.[16][19][75][76] Brian Edwards’s book on modern airport terminals identifies Dulles as an early classic of modern airport architecture and as a turning point in purpose-designed jet-age terminals, noting its monumental single hall, long-span cable-supported roof with inclined concrete supports, continuous glass enclosure, and reliance on mobile lounges; the book also comments on how the mobile-lounge concept later encountered efficiency challenges as traffic grew.[19][75]

Contemporary media coverage has continued to treat the main terminal as a widely revered design. A Simple Flying article describes the terminal as “famous” and “widely revered,” summarizing its distinctive roof form and the mobile-lounge concept and presenting it as a defining feature of Washington Dulles International Airport.[77] Other popular histories, such as a WTTW feature associated with the television series "10 Buildings That Changed America," profile Dulles as a significant American building completed in 1962, emphasizing the catenary-curve roof, the openness of the interior hall, and the innovative use of mobile lounges, though the series context itself is not fully detailed in the available material.[15]

While these sources consistently portray the Dulles main terminal as architecturally important and culturally symbolic, the available evidence in this set does not provide systematic data on public-opinion surveys, detailed preservation-policy debates, or specific media appearances beyond these profiles. The article therefore focuses on documented institutional recognitions (such as awards and eligibility determinations) and representative critical appraisals from architectural and cultural commentators.[2][16][19][21][26]
